content:
The best workflow note is not documentation. It is mercy for the person who has to return.

8 min read
Just now

--

I came back from two weeks in Europe with the familiar mixture of clarity and administrative confusion.
The suitcase was unpacked. The laundry had developed opinions. My calendar had resumed its small campaign of legal intimidation. And Codex had been upgraded while I was away.
This is the sort of thing that can make a person feel briefly displaced inside their own work.
The tool is newer. The interface has changed in small ways. A few capabilities have improved. Meanwhile, the projects you left behind are sitting exactly where you left them, with their half-made decisions, their particular standards, and their extremely unreasonable belief that you will remember why a folder called final-final-actually-use-this-one contains three almost identical files.
But something lovely happened when I returned.
The workflow was still there.
Not only the folders and drafts. The taste was still there. The reasons behind the choices were still there. The boundaries were still there. A fashion prompt still needed to begin from the pressure of the heel, not a pile of decorative clothing words. A Medium essay still needed to be warm without opening the private room too far. A useful AI collaborator still needed to help with the work…
